additionalTime: 2 to 8 hours steak marinade, 10 minutes steak rest, plus 10 minutes potato steam-dry time
author: Robert Carr
description: Carne asada burritos with citrus-garlic marinated steak, savory Mexican-style rice, creamy pinto beans, crispy seasoned potatoes, Oaxaca-Jack cheese, avocado crema, quick pickled onions, a toasted flour tortilla, and roasted arbol-tomatillo hot sauce on the side.

groceryIngredients.item: flap steak, skirt steak, or flank steak
Oaxaca cheese or low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella

ingredients: 2 pounds flap steak, skirt steak, or flank steak
2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the steak, or 1 1/4 teaspoons Morton kosher salt
1/3 cup fresh orange juice, from about 2 oranges
1/4 cup fresh lime juice for the marinade, from about 3 limes
3 tablespoons avocado oil for the marinade and steak, divided
1 tablespoon Worcestershire sauce
1 teaspoon low-sodium soy sauce
1 tablespoon minced chipotle pepper in adobo
5 cloves garlic for the marinade, finely grated or minced
1 teaspoon cumin for the marinade
1 teaspoon Mexican oregano
1 teaspoon black pepper for the marinade
1 teaspoon brown sugar
1 cup long-grain white rice
1 tablespoon avocado oil for the rice
2 tablespoons finely minced white onion for the rice
1 clove garlic for the rice, finely minced
1 tablespoon tomato paste for the rice
1 3/4 cups chicken broth for the rice
1 bay leaf, optional
1/2 teaspoon cumin for the rice
1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the rice, or 1/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
2 tablespoons finely chopped cilantro for the rice
1 tablespoon fresh lime juice for the rice
2 15-ounce cans pinto beans, drained but not rinsed
1/2 cup chicken broth or water for the beans
1 tablespoon avocado oil for the beans
1/2 small white onion for the beans, finely diced
1 clove garlic for the beans, minced
1 teaspoon cumin for the beans
1/2 teaspoon black pepper for the beans
1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the beans, or 1/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1 1/4 pounds Yukon Gold potatoes, cut into 1/2-inch cubes
1 tablespoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for parboiling the potatoes
1/4 teaspoon baking soda for parboiling the potatoes
2 tablespoons avocado oil for crisping the potatoes
1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika for the potatoes
1/2 teaspoon garlic powder for the potatoes
1/2 teaspoon cumin for the potatoes
3/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the potatoes, or 1/2 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1/4 teaspoon black pepper for the potatoes
1 avocado
1/3 cup sour cream
2 tablespoons fresh lime juice for the avocado crema
1/4 cup cilantro for the avocado crema
1 small garlic clove for the avocado crema
1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the avocado crema
1 small red onion, thinly sliced
1/3 cup apple cider vinegar for the pickled onions
1/3 cup water for the pickled onions
1 tablespoon granulated sugar
1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the pickled onions
4 large burrito-size flour tortillas, preferably 12 inches
4 ounces Oaxaca cheese, freshly shredded, or low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella
4 ounces Monterey Jack cheese, freshly shredded
4 tomatillos, husked and rinsed
2 Roma tomatoes
3 jalapenos
1/2 small white onion for the hot sauce
6 cloves garlic for the hot sauce
8 dried chile de arbol peppers, stems removed
1 teaspoon tomato paste for the hot sauce
1/4 cup apple cider vinegar for the hot sauce
2 tablespoons fresh lime juice for the hot sauce
2 to 5 tablespoons water for the hot sauce
3 tablespoons avocado oil for the hot sauce
1 teaspoon honey
3/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the hot sauce, or 1/2 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
3/4 teaspoon black pepper for the hot sauce
instructions: Trim any large pieces of silver skin from the 2 pounds flap steak, skirt steak, or flank steak, then pat the steak very dry with paper towels.
Season both sides of the steak with 2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt, or 1 1/4 teaspoons Morton kosher salt.

In a bowl or zip-top bag, mix 1/3 cup fresh orange juice, 1/4 cup fresh lime juice, 2 tablespoons avocado oil, 1 tablespoon Worcestershire sauce, 1 teaspoon low-sodium soy sauce, 1 tablespoon minced chipotle pepper in adobo, 5 grated or minced garlic cloves, 1 teaspoon cumin, 1 teaspoon Mexican oregano, 1 teaspoon black pepper, and 1 teaspoon brown sugar.
Add the salted steak to the marinade, turn until coated, cover, and refrigerate for at least 2 hours or up to 8 hours.

Make the quick pickled onions by combining 1 thinly sliced small red onion, 1/3 cup apple cider vinegar, 1/3 cup water, 1 tablespoon granulated sugar, and 1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt in a bowl.
Stir the pickled onions well, press them down into the liquid, and let them sit while the rest of the recipe is prepared.
Make the roasted arbol-tomatillo drip hot sauce by placing 4 tomatillos, 2 Roma tomatoes, 3 jalapenos, 1/2 small white onion, and 6 garlic cloves in a dry cast-iron skillet over medium-high heat.
Cook the hot sauce vegetables for 8 to 12 minutes, turning occasionally, until the tomatillos soften, the garlic is browned in spots, and the tomatoes, jalapenos, and onion are charred in places.
Transfer the roasted vegetables to a blender.
Toast 8 dried chile de arbol peppers in the dry skillet for 15 to 30 seconds, just until fragrant and slightly darkened, then transfer them to the blender.

Add 1 teaspoon tomato paste, 1/4 cup apple cider vinegar, 2 tablespoons fresh lime juice, 2 tablespoons water, 1 teaspoon honey, 3/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 3/4 teaspoon black pepper to the blender.
Blend the hot sauce until smooth, then slowly blend in 3 tablespoons avocado oil so the sauce lightly coats a bite.
Add 1 to 3 tablespoons more water only if needed to make the hot sauce loose enough to drip from a spoon.
Taste the hot sauce and adjust with a small pinch of salt, a few drops of lime juice, or a few drops of honey only if needed.
For a smoother drip sauce, pass the hot sauce through a fine-mesh strainer; for a more rustic sauce, leave it unstrained.
Rinse 1 cup long-grain white rice under cool water until the water runs mostly clear, then drain well.
Heat 1 tablespoon avocado oil in a medium saucepan over medium heat.
Add the drained rice and toast for 2 to 3 minutes, stirring often, until the rice smells nutty and some grains turn lightly opaque.
Add 2 tablespoons finely minced white onion and 1 finely minced garlic clove, then cook for 30 seconds.
Add 1 tablespoon tomato paste and 1/2 teaspoon cumin, then stir for 30 seconds so the tomato paste darkens slightly.
Add 1 3/4 cups chicken broth, 1 bay leaf if using, and 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Bring the rice to a boil, stir once, cover, reduce the heat to low, and cook for 15 minutes.
Turn off the heat and let the rice steam, covered and undisturbed, for 10 minutes.
Remove the bay leaf, fluff the rice, then fold in 2 tablespoons finely chopped cilantro and 1 tablespoon fresh lime juice.
Make the creamy pinto beans by heating 1 tablespoon avocado oil in a small saucepan over medium heat.
Add 1/2 small finely diced white onion and cook for 3 to 4 minutes, until softened.
Add 1 minced garlic clove, 1 teaspoon cumin, 1/2 teaspoon black pepper, and 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, then cook for 30 seconds.
Add 2 drained 15-ounce cans pinto beans and 1/2 cup chicken broth or water.
Simmer the beans for 6 to 8 minutes, stirring often, until creamy but not soupy.
Mash about half of the beans against the side of the pan so the mixture becomes spreadable and can act as the burrito glue.
Make the crispy potatoes by placing 1 1/4 pounds 1/2-inch Yukon Gold potato cubes in a saucepan and covering them with cold water by 1 inch.
Add 1 tablespoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt and 1/4 teaspoon baking soda to the potato water.
Bring the potatoes to a boil, then simmer for 5 to 6 minutes, until the outsides are slightly tender but the centers are not falling apart.
Drain the potatoes very well, spread them on a rimmed baking sheet, and let them steam dry for 10 minutes.

Toss the dried potatoes with 2 tablespoons avocado oil, 1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika, 1/2 teaspoon garlic powder, 1/2 teaspoon cumin, 3/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/4 teaspoon black pepper.
Heat a cast-iron skillet over medium-high heat and add the seasoned potatoes in a single layer.
Cook the potatoes for 10 to 14 minutes, turning every few minutes, until crisp and browned on several sides.

Make the avocado crema by blending or mashing 1 avocado, 1/3 cup sour cream, 2 tablespoons fresh lime juice, 1/4 cup cilantro, 1 small garlic clove, and 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt until smooth.
Remove the steak from the marinade and scrape off any heavy garlic or herb clumps so they do not burn.
Pat the steak very dry with paper towels; this is important for browning instead of steaming.
Heat a cast-iron skillet or grill pan over medium-high to high heat until very hot.
Add a thin film of avocado oil only if the pan looks dry.
Sear the steak for 3 to 5 minutes per side, depending on thickness, until deeply browned and the center reaches 130 to 135 degrees F for medium-rare or 140 to 145 degrees F for medium.
For USDA guidance, cook beef steaks to 145 degrees F with a 3-minute rest; for medium-rare preference, use fresh intact steak and cook to 130 to 135 degrees F if that is your desired doneness.
Transfer the steak to a cutting board and rest for 10 minutes.
Slice the rested steak thinly against the grain, then cut the slices into bite-size pieces so the burrito is easy to bite through.
Mix 4 ounces freshly shredded Oaxaca cheese or low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella and 4 ounces freshly shredded Monterey Jack cheese in a bowl.
Warm each 12-inch flour tortilla in a dry skillet for 20 to 30 seconds per side, or until soft, flexible, and lightly toasted in spots.
Assemble 1 burrito at a time by placing a warm tortilla on a clean work surface.
Spread about 1/3 cup creamy pinto beans just below the center of the tortilla, leaving a wide border on the sides.
Add about 1/3 cup Mexican-style rice over the beans.
Add about 1/3 cup crispy potatoes over the rice.
Add one-quarter of the sliced carne asada over the potatoes.
Sprinkle 2 ounces Oaxaca-Jack cheese over the hot steak.
Add 1 to 2 tablespoons avocado crema and a small forkful of drained pickled onions.
Fold the left and right sides of the tortilla over the filling, then roll tightly from the bottom into a compact burrito.
Place the burrito seam-side down in a dry skillet over medium heat.
Toast the burrito for 1 to 2 minutes per side, starting with the seam side, until lightly crisp, sealed, and browned in spots.
Repeat with the remaining tortillas and fillings.
Serve each toasted burrito with the roasted arbol-tomatillo hot sauce on the side.
Drip a small spoonful of hot sauce onto each bite as you eat instead of pouring it into the burrito, which keeps the tortilla from getting soggy.
notes: Best steak choice: flap steak is the easiest high-reward option because it has strong beef flavor, good fat, and a forgiving texture. Skirt steak is excellent but can be chewier if sliced wrong. Flank steak works well but is leaner.
Do not marinate the steak overnight. The citrus is useful, but too much time in an acidic marinade can make the surface texture mushy.
The marinade keeps Worcestershire as the main savory background note and uses only 1 teaspoon soy sauce so the steak tastes like carne asada instead of steakhouse beef.
The steak must be patted very dry before searing. Wet marinade left on the surface prevents browning and can make the steak taste steamed.
Slice the steak against the grain, then cut it into bite-size pieces. Long strips pull out of the burrito when you bite into it.
The rice is intentionally more savory than plain cilantro-lime rice. Tomato paste, onion, garlic, broth, and cumin make it taste integrated with the burrito instead of like a separate side dish.
The beans should be creamy and spreadable, not loose. They act as a moisture barrier and help hold the burrito together.
The potatoes add crisp texture, absorb steak juices, and make the burrito more satisfying without turning it watery.
Steam-drying the potatoes before crisping matters. Wet potatoes brown poorly and soften faster inside the burrito.

Freshly shredded Oaxaca and Monterey Jack melt better than pre-shredded cheese. If Oaxaca is hard to find, low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella is the best substitute because it gives a similar mild flavor and stretchy melt. Avoid fresh mozzarella here because it is too wet for a burrito.
Drain the pickled onions before adding them to the burrito. Their job is brightness and crunch, not extra liquid.
Use large 12-inch flour tortillas if possible. Smaller tortillas make overstuffed burritos that tear or cannot be rolled tightly.
Toast the assembled burrito seam-side down first. It seals the fold, improves texture, and gives the tortilla a lightly crisp exterior.
The hot sauce is intentionally served outside the burrito. Dripping it onto each bite gives heat, acid, and roasted chile flavor without soaking the tortilla.
Blending the avocado oil into the hot sauce gives it a smooth texture that drips cleanly but still lightly coats each bite.
For a milder hot sauce, use 3 to 4 dried chile de arbol peppers instead of 8 and remove the jalapeno seeds before blending.
For a hotter sauce, use 10 to 12 dried chile de arbol peppers or add 1 serrano with the jalapenos.
The hot sauce should be loose enough to drip but not watery. Add water 1 tablespoon at a time only after blending.
For USDA guidance, cook beef steaks to 145 degrees F with a 3-minute rest. The medium-rare range is included for people who prefer that doneness when using fresh intact steak.
Eat these right after the final skillet toast.
title: Carne Asada Burritos with Crispy Potatoes and Roasted Arbol-Tomatillo Drip Hot Sauce
totalTime: 2 hours 5 minutes plus 2 to 8 hours steak marinade
